coredata.dc:title: Advanced fluorescent carbon dots for quantitative detection of Sudan Red via inner filter effect mechanism

coredata.dc:description: Food safety concerns regarding Sudan Red, a carcinogenic dye, necessitate effective detection methods. This study introduces S, P co-doped blue-emitting carbon dots (BSP-CDs), synthesized via a one-pot hydrothermal method using asparagine as the carbon source. BSP-CDs exhibit excellent optical properties, environmental stability, and selective fluorescence quenching due to the inner filter effect. These properties were utilized to develop a sensitive and rapid “on-off” fluorescence sensing system for Sudan Red detection. The sensor demonstrated a linear detection range of 50–175 μM and a detection limit of 2.11 μM, with high accuracy and recovery rates in real-world water samples. This work offers a green, economical, and highly reproducible approach for Sudan Red monitoring, with significant implications for food safety and environmental protection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Asparagine
Hydrothermal method
Fluorescent carbon dots
Sudan Red detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Application of BSP-CDs as fluorescent probe for Sudan Red detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the hydrothermal synthesis of BSP-CDs.

Structural characterization of BSP-CDs. (a) TEM image, with the upper-left inset showing a high-resolution TEM image; (b) XRD spectrum; (c) FT-IR spectrum; (d) XPS full spectrum; (e-g) High-resolution XPS spectra of C1s, N1s, and O1s elements.

Optical properties of BSP-CDs. (a) Photographs of BSP-CDs under natural light and UV light; (b) Three-dimensional fluorescence spectrum; (c) UV–Vis absorption spectrum (red), excitation spectrum (purple), emission spectrum (blue); (d) Normalized PL emission spectrum of BSP-CDs excited by light of different wavelengths.
Stability measurements of BSP-CDs. (a) pH; (b) Ionic strength; (c) Temperature; (d) Time; (e) Illumination time; (f) Effect of different solvents on the fluorescence intensity of BSP-CDs.
Quantum yield determination of BSP-CDs. (a) Comparison of quantum yields; (b) Plot of integrated fluorescence intensity versus absorbance.

Selectivity and sensitivity test for Sudan Red. (a) Fluorescence intensity after adding different metal ions and antibiotics; (b)Anti-interference test; (c) Fluorescence response of BSP-CDs to different concentrations of Sudan Red; (d) Linear relationship between (I0 − I)/I and Sudan Red concentration in the range of 0.05–0.175 mM.

Proposed detection mechanism study. (a) Comparison of UV–Vis absorption spectra before and after introducing Sudan Red; (b) Excitation and emission spectra of BSP-CDs compared to the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of Sudan Red; (c) Fluorescence lifetime experiment.
Results of the Sudan Red spiking recovery experiment.
Comparison of different fluorescent probes for detecting Sudan Red.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: *CNF: Cellulose Nanofiber; CQDs: Carbon Quantum Dots.

head.ce:title: Advanced fluorescent carbon dots for quantitative detection of Sudan Red via inner filter effect mechanism

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Food safety concerns regarding Sudan Red, a carcinogenic dye, necessitate effective detection methods. This study introduces S, P co-doped blue-emitting carbon dots (BSP-CDs), synthesized via a one-pot hydrothermal method using asparagine as the carbon source. BSP-CDs exhibit excellent optical properties, environmental stability, and selective fluorescence quenching due to the inner filter effect. These properties were utilized to develop a sensitive and rapid “on-off” fluorescence sensing system for Sudan Red detection. The sensor demonstrated a linear detection range of 50–175 μM and a detection limit of 2.11 μM, with high accuracy and recovery rates in real-world water samples. This work offers a green, economical, and highly reproducible approach for Sudan Red monitoring, with significant implications for food safety and environmental protection.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With the improvement of human living standards and continuous advancements in science and technology, food safety issues have increasingly attracted widespread attention from society. Sudan Red, a lipophilic azo dye, forms carcinogenic and mutagenic chemicals such as aniline and naphthol during its degradation process [1,2]. Although Sudan Red is not a food additive but rather an industrial dye, its vibrant and long-lasting color has led to its illegal addition to food products by some unscrupulous vendors. Therefore, accurate detection of Sudan Red in food, pharmaceuticals, and cosmetics has become of critical importance.

Several methods have been developed for the detection of Sudan Red, including high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [3], enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) [4], electrochemical methods (EC) [5], surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) [6], and colorimetric methods [7]. However, these techniques generally suffer from drawbacks such as expensive equipment, complex operation, high costs, and limited applicability. Therefore, it is highly significant to develop a green, non-toxic, and optically stable material for the detection of Sudan Red.

In recent years, fluorescence detection methods have gained considerable attention due to their high sensitivity, low cost, and ease of operation, and have been widely applied for the detection and monitoring of various substances. Among these, fluorescence sensors based on nanomaterials, such as gold nanoclusters [8], silicon nanoparticles, and carbon quantum dots, have seen rapid development. Carbon dots (CDs), also known as carbon quantum dots or carbon nanodots, are zero-dimensional nanomaterials with fluorescence properties and sizes typically less than 10 nm [9]. As an important member of the carbon material family, CDs offer several advantages over traditional organic dyes, metal nanoparticles, and semiconductor quantum dots [10], including low toxicity, unique photoluminescent properties, excellent water solubility, and good biocompatibility. Consequently, CDs have attracted significant interest in a variety of applications, including ion detection [11], sensing[12], anti-counterfeiting [13], biomedical imaging [14], and environmental pollution control [15–17].

In this study, a cost-effective blue-emitting carbon dot (BSP-CD) was successfully synthesized using asparagine as the carbon source via a one-pot hydrothermal approach with S, P co-doping. The carbon dots were characterized by multiple techniques, and their optical properties were evaluated. Due to their outstanding luminescence performance and stability, BSP-CDs were demonstrated to serve as a simple, environmentally friendly fluorescence probe for the detection of Sudan Red in real samples. The sensor exhibits good selectivity for Sudan Red, a broad detection range, and a rapid response time. Moreover, the sensor system shows strong resistance to interference from other substances, highlighting its potential for the rapid determination of trace amounts of Sudan Red in environmental samples.

Asparagine, thiourea, phosphoric acid, methanol (MeOH), ethanol (EtOH), N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), tetrahydrofuran (THF), and acetonitrile (ACN) were purchased from Chengdu Kelong Chemicals Co., Ltd. All metal ions (Fe2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Zn2+, etc.) were obtained from the National Nonferrous Metals and Electronic Materials Analysis and Testing Center. Procaine (PD), ceftriaxone sodium (CRO), glycine (GLY), amoxicillin (AM), and other antibiotics and quinine sulfate (REF) were obtained from Shanghai Aladdin Bio-tech Co., Ltd. All chemicals used were of AR grade and did not require further purification unless otherwise stated. Distilled water was obtained from the School of Chemistry and Materials Science, Sichuan Normal University.

A BRUKER VERTEX 70 Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrometer (USA Nicolet) was used for infrared spectroscopy; a TD-3500 X-ray diffractometer (Dandong Tongda Instrument Co.) was employed to analyze the crystal structure of the carbon dots (XRD); the ESCAPHI 5400 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS) from Thermo Fisher Scientific was used to analyze the surface elemental composition; a 756PC ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometer from Haishenyu Hengping Scientific Instruments was used for absorbance measurements; fluorescence emission and excitation spectra were recorded using a Linptech F98 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Shanghai Linptech Technology Co., Ltd., scan speed 3000 r/min, gain 650 V); a 25 mL hydrothermal reaction kettle from Xiniu Technology Co., Ltd.; a 40 kHz ultrasonic cleaning machine from Shanghai Fuyang Instrument Co., Ltd.; a 101-00AS electric heating constant temperature blast drying oven from Shaoxing Shangcheng Instruments; a SC-2546 desktop low-speed centrifuge from Anhui Zhongjia Scientific Instruments Co., Ltd.

Blue fluorescent carbon dots (denoted BSP-CDs) were synthesized using Asparagine as the main precursor and thiourea and phosphoric acid as dopants through a hydrothermal method. The specific synthesis process is as follows: 0.5 g of asparagine was accurately weighed, then 0.3 g of thiourea and 0.5 mL of phosphoric acid were added to the mixture. These materials were dissolved in 10 mL of ethanol. After stirring to ensure the solution was homogeneous, the mixture was subjected to ultrasonic treatment for 10 min to enhance dissolution and dispersion. The resulting mixture was transferred into a Teflon-lined high-pressure reactor and heated at 180 °C for 8 h to complete the carbon dot synthesis reaction. After the reaction, the system was naturally cooled to room temperature. The supernatant was collected and filtered using a 0.22 μm microporous filter membrane to remove large particles. The dialysis filtrate was then filtered with a molecular weight cutoff of 500 Da in deionized water for 12 h to further remove small molecule impurities and thus separate the purified CDs. The final BSP-CDs solution was stored at 4 °C for further experiments and applications. The entire preparation process is illustrated in the Fig. 1 .

All experiments were performed under standard room temperature and pressure conditions, aiming to investigate the effect of Sudan Red on the fluorescence quenching of BSP-CDs. The procedure is summarized as follows: First, 30 μL of BSP-CDs solution was added to 2 mL of distilled water, and its initial fluorescence intensity was measured using a fluorescence spectrometer, denoted as I0. Then, Sudan Red solutions of varying concentrations (ranging from 0 μM to 175 μM) were added to separate cuvettes, and the mixtures were agitated to ensure thorough mixing. The fluorescence intensity of each solution was then measured and recorded.

The morphology and chemical structure of BSP-CDs were analyzed using transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The TEM image of BSP-CDs (Fig. 2 a) shows that they exhibit a granular morphology, with an average particle size of approximately 8–10 nm. The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image reveals a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, which corresponds to the (100) plane of graphene [18]. To assess the surface crystallinity of the synthesized carbon dots, XRD characterization was performed. A clear broad diffraction peak at 2θ = 23.96° (Fig. 2b) corresponds to the (002) plane of graphite [19]. The FTIR spectrum of BSP-CDs (Fig. 2c) shows a broad peak at 3449 cm−1, attributed to O–H/N–H stretching vibrations; a peak at 2354 cm−1 corresponds to C≡C stretching vibrations; a sharp peak at 1714 cm−1 is related to CO/CC stretching vibrations; a strong peak at 1385 cm−1 is associated with C–H in-plane bending vibrations; a peak at 1188 cm−1 corresponds to C–C stretching vibrations. Additionally, a peak at 1020 cm−1 indicates P-O stretching vibrations, and a peak at 640 cm−1 corresponds to C-S/C-P stretching vibrations [20], confirming the successful incorporation of S and P dopants and the presence of hydrophilic groups such as amino, hydroxyl, and carboxyl groups on the surface of BSP-CDs.

To further confirm the chemical structure and elemental composition of BSP-CDs, XPS analysis was performed. The full XPS spectrum of BSP-CDs (Fig. 2d) shows characteristic peaks at 533.2 eV, 399.8 eV, and 286.4 eV, corresponding to O1s, N1s, and C1s, respectively, indicating that the carbon dots primarily consist of C, O, and N elements, with atomic percentages of 62.86 %, 26.09 %, and 9.12 %, respectively. Small amounts of S and P elements were also detected. The S2p peak likely originates from thiourea, and the P2p peak may be due to oxidation during the high-temperature, high-pressure reaction with phosphoric acid. The C1s spectrum (Fig. 2e) was fitted to reveal four peaks at 284.12 eV, 285.30 eV, 287.83 eV, and 287.88 eV, corresponding to CC/CC, CN, CO, and CO functional groups, respectively [21]. The N1s spectrum (Fig. 2f) showed deconvoluted peaks at 399.52 eV (N-S/N-P), 399.66 eV (N-C), and 402.38 eV (NH), indicating that nitrogen is mainly present in the forms of pyridine nitrogen and amino nitrogen [22]. The O1s spectrum (Fig. 2g) exhibited peaks at 531.23 eV, 532.75 eV, and 533.32 eV, which are attributed to OC, OC, and OP/OP bonds, respectively [3]. These results, combined with the FTIR data, indicate that the surface of the carbon dots contains OH, CC, COOH, and NH2 groups, and successfully incorporates S and P dopants.

The optical properties of BSP-CDs were studied in neutral aqueous solution (pH = 7) at room temperature using UV–vis spectroscopy and fluorescence spectroscopy. Fig. 3 a displays photographs of BSP-CDs under natural light and UV light. In natural light, the BSP-CDs solution is optically transparent (colorless to pale yellow at high concentrations), while bright blue fluorescence is observed under UV excitation. The 3D excitation-emission spectra of BSP-CDs are shown in Fig. 3b. BSP-CDs exhibit distinct excitation and emission peaks, with UV–vis absorption (Fig. 3c) showing a strong absorption band at 247 nm, which is typical of aromatic phenyl ring absorption, attributed to the π → π* electronic transition of sp2 hybridized carbon atoms [23]. The excitation and emission spectra indicate that the optimal excitation and emission wavelengths for BSP-CDs are 382 nm and 467 nm, respectively. These results confirm that BSP-CDs possess excellent fluorescence properties. The data of excitation-dependence experiments were normalized in Fig. 3d, it can be seen that when the excitation wavelength increased from 340 nm to 390 nm, the fluorescence emission peak position of BSP-CDs changed significantly with the excitation wavelength, similar to previous studies, indicating that BSP-CDs had the excitation wavelength dependence and carbon points belonging to the quantum state, possibly related to the oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of BSP-CDs. [24].

As fluorescent probes, CDs are intended for use in various environments. Therefore, the photoluminescence (PL) stability is critical for their application in chemical sensing. We tested several typical external factors that could influence the photoluminescence of CDs, including pH, ionic strength, temperature, solvent polarity, light exposure time, and storage duration. As reported in the literature [25], pH can influence the fluorescence intensity of CDs by affecting the surface charge state. Solutions were prepared using hydrochloric acid and sodium hydroxide to investigate the stability of CDs across a pH range of 1–13. As shown in Fig. 4
a, fluorescence intensity remained stable within the pH range of 3–11, while significant quenching occurred only under strongly acidic or alkaline conditions. This indicates that the CDs exhibit one of their characteristic stable behaviors in a neutral to mildly alkaline environment, and thus, subsequent experiments were conducted at pH=8. Fig. 4 b demonstrates the stability of the fluorescence intensity of the CDs as the NaCl concentration was gradually increased from 0 mM to 500 mM. The fluorescence intensity remained largely unchanged, indicating that the CDs possess excellent tolerance to ionic strength and can adapt to different environmental conditions. Temperature is another critical factor influencing the photoluminescence of CDs. As shown in Fig. 4 c, fluorescence intensity changed minimally when the temperature ranged from 25 °C to 80 °C. Even at elevated temperatures, no quenching was observed, suggesting that the blue carbon dots synthesized in this study have outstanding thermal stability, which is essential for their application in high-temperature environments. Further, when the CDs were stored at 4 °C for 7 days, their fluorescence intensity remained almost unchanged (Fig. 4 d), demonstrating that these CDs are suitable for long-term storage and use, offering significant economic benefits. Although prolonged light exposure can enhance the fluorescence intensity of CDs, exceeding a certain threshold can damage the carbon dots and cause a sharp decrease in fluorescence intensity. To investigate their resistance to photobleaching, the CDs solution was exposed to continuous UV light (365 nm) for 120 min in a UV lamp box. Fluorescence intensity was measured every 20 min, and the results shown in Fig. 4 e revealed no significant change in fluorescence intensity during the 120-minute exposure, indicating that the CDs synthesized in this study exhibit excellent photostability.

To examine the effect of solvent polarity on the fluorescence of the CDs, we tested six common solvents: water (WAT), methanol (MTH), ethanol (ETH), tetrahydrofuran (THF), N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF), and acetonitrile (ACN). The fluorescence spectra shown in Fig. 4 f reveal that the fluorescence intensity of BSP-CDs is similar across all six solvents, with the maximum emission peak unchanged. This indicates that solvent polarity significantly influences the fluorescence performance of the CDs. To further investigate the effect of different solvents on the photoluminescence properties of the CDs, we measured the quantum yield (QY) of the blue carbon dots in various solvents [26]. A linear fitting was performed by plotting the integrated fluorescence intensity against the absorbance, allowing the quantum yield in each solvent to be determined, as shown in Fig. 5 a. The quantum yields of BSP-CDs in the six solvents were found to be as follows: MTH: 11.5 %, ETH: 15.4 %, water: 12.5 %, THF: 11.4 %, DMF: 14.2 %, and ACN: 9.3 % as shown in Fig. 5b. Therefore, the fluorescence properties of the CDs are closely related to the solvent polarity, and the solvents that exhibited the best fluorescence emission performance also showed the highest quantum yield. Therefore, selecting an appropriate solvent can further enhance the photoluminescent efficiency of the CDs, which opens up broader possibilities for their applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of BSP-CDs as fluorescent probe for Sudan Red detection

ce:section.ce:para: Building on the optical properties and stability of BSP-CDs described earlier, we applied them for the detection of Sudan Red. The fluorescence intensity of BSP-CDs was monitored as Sudan Red was gradually added to a solution at pH=8. However, since Sudan Red is commonly found in complex systems such as wastewater, the selectivity of the probe is critical. To assess the selectivity of BSP-CDs for Sudan Red, we investigated the effect of various potential interferents on the fluorescence signal of BSP-CDs, including 13 common metal ions (Fe2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Ag+, Hg2+, K+, etc.) and 22 antibiotics (such as PD, CRO, GLY, AM, UREA, etc.). All metal ions and antibiotics were prepared at a concentration of 5 μmol/L.

As shown in Fig. 6
a (PL emission spectra), exposure of BSP-CDs to different metal ions and antibiotics resulted in either quenching or a slight enhancement of the fluorescence intensity of the blue carbon dots. In most cases, the fluorescence intensity (I) decreased, while only a few substances caused a slight increase in the PL intensity. Notably, only Sudan Red (SD) caused a significant quenching of the fluorescence, indicating that BSP-CDs exhibit excellent selectivity for Sudan Red. This suggests their potential for detecting Sudan Red in complex real-world samples. A systematic study of the interaction of BSP-CDs with a variety of disrupactors (including metal ions and antibiotics) in the presence of Sudan red, as shown in Figure b. Notably, the coexistence of multiple metal ions and antibiotics did not significantly interfere with the fluorescence intensity of BSP-CDs, which further confirms the highly specific Sudan red recognition ability of BSP-CDs. This finding highlights the significant advantage of BSP-CDs in detecting Sudan red in complex environmental samples, maintaining high sensitivity and specificity response to the target even under the coexistence of distractors, providing an important basis for their application in real sample analysis.

Sensitivity is another crucial parameter for evaluating the performance of a fluorescent probe. As shown in Fig. 6 c, the fluorescence peak intensity of BSP-CDs systematically decreased with increasing Sudan Red concentration (0–175 μM). Fig. 6 d presents the linear fitting of the fluorescence intensity of BSP-CDs versus Sudan Red concentration, showing a strong linear relationship between Sudan Red concentration and fluorescence intensity in the range of 50–175 μM. The linear regression equation is:
I o
- I
I o
= 1.93776 c + 0.7836

Where I0 represents the fluorescence intensity of the blank sample; I represents the fluorescence intensity of the antibiotic / metal ion; c represents the concentration of the antibiotic / metal ion in μM; the left side of the equation represents the relative change of the fluorescence intensity, reflecting the quenching efficiency of the fluorescence signal by the substance to be measured, the correlation coefficient R2 = 0.9929. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated using the 3σ rule (LOD = 3σ/K), where σ is the relative standard deviation of ten replicate fluorescence measurements of the blank solution, and K is the slope of the calibration curve. The LOD of Sudan Red using BSP-CDs was found to be 2.11 μM, with a detection range of 50–175 μM. Comparing this method with other fluorescent detection methods for Sudan Red (see Table 2), the BSP-CDs sensor exhibits superior sensitivity and a wide detection range.

To further evaluate the potential of the synthesized BSP-CDs (carbon dots) in practical applications, we applied them to the quantitative analysis of Sudan Red (SD) in distilled water and tap water, performing spiking and recovery experiments to verify accuracy. As shown in Table 1
, for the distilled water samples, the recovery ranged from 99.75 % to 104.14 %, with relative standard deviations (RSD, n = 3) between 0.84 % and 1.22 %. In tap water samples, the recovery ranged from 98.24 % to 105.26 %, with RSDs between 0.79 % and 1.00 %. These results confirm the high accuracy of the proposed method. Therefore, the method developed in this study not only offers high sensitivity but also demonstrates excellent reliability and reproducibility, making it suitable for detecting Sudan Red residues in food.

Furthermore, to comprehensively evaluate the sensor’s performance, we compared its linear range and detection limit with other Sudan Red detection methods reported in the literature. The results, summarized in Table 2, show that the BSP-CDs fluorescent sensor offers several advantages, including a wide detection range, high precision, low sample consumption, and ease of operation. It holds significant potential for detecting Sudan Red in real samples.

The fluorescence detection mechanism encompasses various phenomena, including photo-induced electron transfer (PET) [31], dynamic quenching, static quenching, fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) [32], and inner filter effects (IFE) [33]. In this study, we investigated the fluorescence detection mechanism involved in the interaction between Sudan Red and BSP-CDs. Firstly, by comparing the UV–visible absorption spectra of BSP-CDs before and after the addition of Sudan Red (shown in Fig. 7
a), we observed that the two spectra almost completely overlapped. This suggests that no new compounds were formed, ruling out the possibility of static quenching. To further confirm this, we conducted fluorescence lifetime experiments. As seen in Fig. 7 b, the excitation and emission spectra of BSP-CDs significantly overlap with the UV–visible absorption spectrum of Sudan Red. This overlap strongly suggests that the quenching effect may arise from the inner filter effect (IFE). Additionally, the time-resolved fluorescence decay spectra shown in Fig. 7 c further support this hypothesis. After introducing Sudan Red, the average fluorescence lifetime of BSP-CDs remained nearly unchanged, with calculated lifetimes of 3.799 ns and 3.791 ns, respectively, confirming the occurrence of an inner filter effect. Based on the theoretical analysis and experimental results, we have developed an “on-off” type fluorescence sensing system for Sudan Red detection using BSP-CDs, which effectively enables quantitative detection with good potential for practical applications.

In this study, we synthesized S, P co-doped blue carbon dots (BSP-CDs) using asparagine as the carbon source via a solvothermal method. A comprehensive characterization of the BSP-CDs was performed using various techniques, including FT-IR, XRD, XPS, UV–vis, and fluorescence spectroscopy, to analyze their crystal structure, functional group composition, and optical properties. The results indicate that the BSP-CDs primarily consist of a graphite-like carbon core and surface luminescent units, exhibiting a uniform size distribution. The surface of the BSP-CDs is rich in functional groups such as carboxyl (–COOH) and amino (NH2) groups, which contribute to their excellent water solubility.

Furthermore, we conducted an in-depth investigation into the photoluminescence mechanism of BSP-CDs and developed a novel, highly efficient detection method based on the inner filter effect. This method demonstrates high sensitivity toward Sudan Red, with a simple and rapid preparation process. The sensor exhibits a linear response range of 50 μM to 175 μM and a detection limit of 2.11 μM. It serves as an effective tool for both qualitative and quantitative analysis of Sudan Red, enabling sensitive detection in real samples with high recovery rates. The proposed method demonstrates significant practical potential and broad application prospects, offering a new approach for the effective analysis of Sudan Red in environmental samples.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Thank Prof. Keqing Zhao in College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Sichuan Normal University, Prof. Dongmao Zhang, Ph.D Joanna Xiuzhu Xu, Xue Leng et al. in Department of Chemistry, Mississippi State University for helpful advices.
